name: Galactic small-scale structure challenges to LambdaCDM
summary: Cold dark matter N-body simulations under LambdaCDM predict steep central density cusps (rho ~ r^-1) in dwarf galaxies, large numbers of low-mass satellite galaxies, and several massive subhalos that should host visible galaxies but do not. Observations show flat cores (rho ~ const) in dwarf rotation curves, far fewer satellites than predicted, and the most massive predicted subhalos appear to be empty ('too-big-to-fail'). Either baryonic feedback inside collisionless CDM resolves this, or DM has non-CDM properties (warm DM, self-interacting DM, fuzzy DM).

characteristic_numbers.expected: CDM N-body predicts rho ~ r^-1 (NFW cusp)
characteristic_numbers.note: Local-Group dwarf rotation curves systematically flatter than CDM-only simulations; difference at 1-10 kpc scale.
Pure-CDM simulations overpredict; ultra-faint dwarfs from DES and LSST are adding to count but not closing the gap.

characteristic_numbers.expected: all 'too-big-to-fail' subhalos should host visible galaxies
characteristic_numbers.note: Massive subhalos around MW with V_max > 30 km/s should be hard to keep dark.

principles_stressed.note: Possibilities: (a) baryonic feedback (SN-driven turbulence, AGN) flattens cusps and suppresses small galaxies in standard CDM - active area of simulation work; (b) DM is self-interacting at sigma/m ~ 0.1-1 cm^2/g (resolves all three); (c) DM is warm/fuzzy and suppresses small-scale power; (d) systematic in observations. Status: significantly relieved by improved feedback but not closed.

history: Core-cusp: Flores-Primack (1994), Moore (1994). Missing satellites: Klypin-Kravtsov-Valenzuela-Prada (1999); Moore et al. (1999). Too-big-to-fail: Boylan-Kolchin-Bullock-Kaplinghat (2011). Comprehensive review: Bullock and Boylan-Kolchin (2017), 'Small-scale challenges to the LambdaCDM paradigm', Annu. Rev. Astron. Astrophys. 55, 343, arXiv:1707.04256.

references.citation: Bullock and Boylan-Kolchin, 'Small-scale challenges to the LambdaCDM paradigm', Annu. Rev. Astron. Astrophys. 55, 343 (2017)
